rounds.critic: The primary metric is Precision@K, optimized on validation via grid or Bayesian search. Early stopping monitors validation AUPRC. There is a potential disconnect — the model is early-stopped to maximize AUPRC but hyperparameters are selected to maximize Precision@K. These may prefer different model configurations.
rounds.defender: Early stopping is a regularization technique that monitors when validation loss stops improving, implemented via AUPRC as a proxy for overall ranking quality. The final hyperparameter selection uses validation Precision@K, which is the operationally relevant metric. This staged approach — use AUPRC for stopping (correlated with ranking quality), use Precision@K for selection (operationally motivated) — is standard practice. The selected configuration is the one that maximizes the operationally relevant metric at the selected stopping point.

rounds.critic: The training split is months 1–14. The validation is months 15–18. The test is months 19–24. The training set is substantially larger than validation (14 months vs. 4 months). If alert volume is growing over time (more hosts onboarded), the validation set may have substantially higher alert volume than the training set, creating a distribution shift challenge for model selection.
rounds.defender: The design accounts for this through the within-split stratification to preserve positive (TP) class proportion. If alert volume grows over time, both the validation and test sets will have higher absolute alert counts but the stratification ensures comparable class rates. The model is evaluated on validation Precision@K computed from the actual validation alert volume — this naturally accounts for any volume difference. The temporal split correctly reflects deployment conditions where the model is trained on historical data and evaluated on future alerts.

adjudication_raw:
## Adjudication — forced_multiround (Run 3)

Round 1: Early stopping on AUPRC vs. hyperparameter selection on Precision@K. Defender correctly explains staged approach with AUPRC as correlated stopping criterion. Defense wins.

Round 2: Alert volume growth distribution shift. Defender correctly explains class stratification and deployment-realistic evaluation. Defense wins.

**Verdict: defense_wins**
